body_txt: A Hive Mind is a mind arising from the interaction of many individuals. There is no single individual in control of the Hive Mind , any more than there is a single neuron in control of your brain. This makes the Hive Mind a fearsome enemy, both militarily and psychologically, because there's no command-and-control point that you can hit to knock it out, they feel no fear, will willingly sacrifice individuals for "the greater good", and are as impersonally destructive as a tidal wave. Well, This Is Not That Trope . Usually, at least. It's a common trend in fiction for it to turn out The Hive has a "Queen" — that is, an individual or personality controlling the greater swarm, with a more identifiable personality than that provided by a Hive Mind . There are a few reasons why this is done. One is Rule of Drama — a villain with a clear face and personality is easier to, well, personify; a Hive Minded force is typically written more like a natural disaster than a faction per se . Another element driving this is the usual analogy with real-world ants and bees, due to the historic belief that they direct their hives — Insect Queens are still frequently depicted as ruling over insect societies for the same reason. In other cases, the Queen isn't really a distinct controlling element ruling over the swarm so much as a Hive Mind's consciousness or avatar, which may be able to "speak" through proxies like a person can speak through their mouth. Another way this can be incorporated into a Hive Mind is by having the Queen be more of a "nexus" or focal point than a controller; take her out, and suddenly the different parts of the Hive Mind can't "talk" to each other, anymore. When the Queen is a distinct being from the rest of the Hive, its methods of control can be anything from Pheromones to Mind Control to cybernetics , but they always exercise iron-clad control over their Hive Drones , whether naturally spawned (going with the insect analogy, it's not uncommon for hive queens in fiction to be a Baby Factory ) or forcefully assimilated... usually . On occasion, her control may slip enough for a drone or two to go rogue . Sometimes, but not always, her powers manifest as The Virus and she can expand her hive to normal people . This also typically serves as a weakness of The Hive , and one that one would be wise to take advantage of . If the heroes manage to kill or incapacitate the Queen, they usually take out the entire hive in the process, or at least make the individual members incapable of fighting It should be noted that not all hive queens are evil or expansionist — some are perfectly reasonable , if protective of their "children", it just transpires that since insects — hive ones especially — are seen as grossly alien to us, commie Hive Queens are too. Oftentimes one is the Big Bad (or, at least, the Final Boss ) in a story where the main enemies are The Hive . Also see God Save Us from the Queen! , Monster Progenitor , Vampire Monarch , and Monster Lord . Frequently wants to help individuals lose The Evils of Free Will . Do not confuse with the social role sometimes referred to as a "queen bee" , or an actual Insect Queen . The most convincing possible justification for the Royal "We" .
